data.title: Axel Springer buys the UK's Telegraph newspaper for $770M, plans U.S. expansion
data.description:
German media company Axel Springer has bought Telegraph Media Group, which operates the UK's Daily Telegraph newspaper, for $770M, according to a public statement on Friday.

The newspaper was purchased from RedBird IMI, which itself is a joint venture between U.S. private equity firm RedBird Capital Partners and the UAE's media group IMI.

Axel Springer said it will back an investment program to grow Telegraph into becoming "the leading center-right media outlet in the English-speaking world" and is also working aggressively to expand the publication's reach in the U.S. market.

To support its U.S. expansion plans, Axel said it plans to leverage expertise from its currently owned brands Politico and Business Insider.

"More than 20 years ago, we tried to acquire The Telegraph and did not succeed. Now our dream comes true. To be the owner of this institution of quality British journalism is a privilege and a duty," said Axel Springer CEO Mathias Döpfner.

The parties are currently awaiting the UK government's approval for clearing the transaction. 

The CEO of Axel Springer currently owns 95% of the company. KKR (KKR) and CPP Investments were previously major shareholders until January 2025; they relinquished control of Axel's media portfolio but are currently the top stakeholders in its classifieds business.

data.title: Autoliv taps EMEA finance executive as new CFO
data.description:
• Autoliv (ALV) has appointed Monika Grama as the company’s new chief financial officer, succeeding Fredrik Westin who is leaving the company on March 31.
  • Westin announced last July his decision to leave the company to pursue a position in continental Europe.
  • Grama is currently vice president of finance for the company’s EMEA division and has been with the company since 2009.
  • Shares of the Swedish airbag manufacturer are in the midst of an eight-day losing streak with a cumulative loss of 11% in value.

data.title: February's weak jobs number is an 'outlier,' NEC's Hassett says
data.description:
White House National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said on Friday that a couple of “one-off” factors hurt the February nonfarm payrolls number, such as “crazy weather” and labor strikes in the West.

"I think that, on net, it's almost a wash number," he said during an interview on Bloomberg Television. He also believes that a new birth/death model that the Bureau of  Labor Statistics is using "is going to make the number more volatile."

Earlier on Friday, the BLS estimated that the  U.S. economy lost 92K jobs, a shock to markets considering the consensus estimate was for adding 60K. The January number was revised down by 4K to +126K. The biggest job losses in February came from healthcare — usually one of the strongest sectors for job creation — information, and the federal government.

Averaging the January and February numbers, "it's at about the rate of job growth consistent with three or four percent GDP growth," Hassett said.

He doesn't expect a string of jobs reports similar to February's because "every other indicator is consistent with very strong GDP growth right now, so this really is an outlier number," he said.

Equity markets are paring losses. The Nasdaq, which had declined as much as 1.7%, trimmed its dip to 0.9%. The S&P 500, down as much as 1.7%, fell 1.1% in midmorning trading. And the Dow, off as much as 2% at one point, moderated to a 1.2% decline.

data.title: Former Palantir employees blocked from poaching, using secrets, judge rules
data.description:
Percepta AI, which was founded by former employees of Palantir Technologies (PLTR), has been blocked from poaching employees from their former employer and using its data, a Manhattan judge ruled.

US District Judge J. Paul Oetken ruled last month that three employees from Percepta AI named in the suit—Hirsh Jain, Radha Jain, and Joanna Cohen—were barred from recruiting staff for the company. However, the 41-page opinion was sealed due in part to confidential business information and certain customer and client data. Instead, he released a one-page order that summarized the findings. Now, several details of the opinion have been made public and indicate that Judge Oetken said Palantir would suffer “irreparable harm” if Percepta continued to take Palantir employees and use its data.

Despite the constraints about poaching Palantir employees, Judge Oetken added that Palantir's non-compete requirement against the defendants, which include Hirsh Jain (Percepta's CEO) and Cohen, a former Palantir engineer, was too broad and “sweeping.” As such, they will be allowed to continue working for Percepta.

Judge Oetken asked Palantir's legal team to choose roles that former employees could hold legally at Percepta, and the legal team responded with “janitor” or “chief operating officer.” As such, Judge Oetken found that unrealistic for people at this stage of their careers.

“This opinion marks a major win for Percepta and for builders everywhere. No one has a monopoly on applied AI,” Hirsh Jain said in a statement. “When massive companies try to sue startups out of existence, they are using their scale to stifle innovation.”

Percepta is owned by venture capital firm General Catalyst and was publicly unveiled in October.

The public details from the judge's findings were first reported by Bloomberg. 

(This story has been updated with additional information.)

data.title: BlackRock curbs withdrawals from private credit fund, adding to broader private credit woes
data.description:
BlackRock (BLK) has curbed withdrawals from its private credit fund after a surge in redemption requests, becoming the latest to be highlighted in the broader private credit issue.

BLK shares were 5.31% lower at $974.58 during Friday morning trading.

Earlier this month, Blackstone (BX) had said its $82B private credit fund's repurchase requests for the quarter exceeded the 5% of shares typically available for repurchase, and the board elected to upsize the offer to 7% of shares, the maximum amount permitted without changing the terms of the repurchase offer.

In February, Blue Owl Capital (OWL) permanently restricted withdrawals from a $1.6B private credit vehicle and sold $1.4B in loans to pension funds and its own insurance company.

The news comes amid growing concerns about systemic risks in the $1.8T private credit market.

HPS Corporate Lending Fund, one of BlackRock's biggest private credit funds, said its shareholders requested 9.3% of their shares in the first quarter of 2026. However, it will be fulfilling the repurchase request for 5% of the shares outstanding as of December 31, 2025. The amount comes to ~$620M.

The New York-based investment manager noted in the HLEND letter that redemption requests exceeded the 5% framework for the first time since its inception.

"We have made this decision, like all prior decisions regarding the fund's capital, with the best interest of all shareholders in mind," the letter said.

"Historically, periods of uncertainty and volatility have created some of the most compelling investment opportunities within private credit markets. We believe that we are entering into that type of environment," said the letter.

HLEND noted it has over $4.4B in liquidity as of February 28, and raised ~$840M of subscriptions in the first quarter.

data.title: Kingstone targets 16%-20% premium growth and California entry in 2026 while projecting $500M direct premiums by 2029
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kingstone Companies, Inc. (KINS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Meryl Golden, President and CEO, reported "our most profitable quarter and year in Kingstone's history," highlighting net income of $14.8 million, diluted earnings per share of $1.03, a GAAP net combined ratio of 64.2%, and an annualized return on equity of 51% for the fourth quarter. For the full year, net income more than doubled to $40.8 million, and diluted earnings per share increased 95% to $2.88.
  • Golden noted, "from year-end 2023 to year-end 2025, we grew direct premiums written 39%, while improving our combined ratio by 30 points," emphasizing the structural nature of these improvements and the effectiveness of the Select product, which now represents 57% of policies in force.
  • The CEO announced Kingstone's entry into California in Q2 2026 on an excess and surplus lines basis, calling it "one of the largest homeowners markets with $15 billion in written premium," and stating that the initial contribution will be "less than 5% of our '26 premium."
  • Golden established a five-year goal: "a 5-year goal of $500 million in direct premiums written by year-end 2029."
  • Guidance changes and a strategic shift to using the underlying combined ratio as the primary operating lens were introduced, with Golden explaining, "we're introducing the underlying combined ratio, which excludes catastrophe losses and prior year reserve development as our primary operating lens."
  • Randy Patten, CFO, stated, "the fourth quarter was our most profitable quarter in the company's history and our ninth consecutive quarter of profitability," reporting net income of $14.8 million and noting, "for the 2026 treaty year, we have further reduced our quota share cession from 16% to 5%, reflecting continued confidence in the quality of our underwriting portfolio and capital position to support our growth."

Outlook

  • Guidance for fiscal year 2026 includes direct premiums written growth of 16% to 20%, an underlying combined ratio (excluding catastrophes and prior year reserve development) of 74% to 76%, a catastrophe loss assumption of 7 to 10 points, and a net combined ratio of 81% to 86%.
  • Diluted earnings per share guidance is $2.20 to $2.90, with a midpoint of $2.55, reflecting an increase relative to the initial outlook.
  • Golden clarified, "the headline year-over-year change in earnings per share and return on equity is driven almost entirely by our assumption of a higher-than-normal catastrophe load not by any deterioration in our underlying business."
  • The company maintains its five-year growth trajectory, aiming for $500 million in direct premiums written by year-end 2029, and plans to refine the outlook as the year unfolds.

Financial Results

  • Direct premiums written were $82.8 million in Q4, growing 14%, and $277.8 million for the full year, a 15% increase.
  • Net premiums earned increased 38% in Q4 and 46% for the year, primarily due to the reduced quota share.
  • The underlying loss ratio improved to 34.7% in Q4, and for the full year, it was 44.4%. The catastrophe loss ratio was 1.2 points for the full year.
  • Book value per diluted share increased 75% to $8.28, and shareholders' equity ended the year at $122.7 million, up 84%.
  • Net investment income for the quarter rose 65% to $3 million, and for the year, it reached $9.8 million, a 44% increase.
  • The expense ratio improved to 30% for the year, down from 41% in 2021.

Q&A

  • Robert Edward Farnam, Brean Capital: Asked about risk differences and strategy for California. Meryl Golden explained Kingstone's methodical approach, stating, "our plan is to enter with the same differentiators as we have in New York...we will be disciplined. Our plan is to enter small, less than 5% of our premium for 2026, make sure we understand the market and we're doing everything right before we expand."
  • Farnam inquired about the California quota share. Golden confirmed, "we have a 30% quota share for California initially."
  • Farnam questioned geographic targeting in California. Golden replied, "we're going to write all across the state...we're focused on low to moderate wildfire risk."
  • Farnam asked about expense ratio targets. Patten responded, "with some economies of scale, we can get that expense ratio down, possibly another half to a full point."
  • McClure (private investor) requested details on exposure limits. Golden stated, "in New York, increased the available coverage A or value of the home to $5 million...that would be our plan for California as well."
  • McClure asked about winter storm claims. Golden replied, "our estimate for the winter storm losses has been included in our guidance for 2026."
  • McClure asked about the California market. Golden highlighted, "the primary issue in California is the regulatory environment...as an E&S writer, we're not subject to that same regulation. So it gives us a real advantage."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, focusing on growth, market expansion, and sustained profitability, as evidenced by congratulations and interest in strategy, with no signs of skepticism or negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing structural improvements and competitive advantages. Golden repeatedly underscored advantages and long-term strategy, stating, "we will continue to execute with discipline, advance our measured expansion road map and allocate capital prudently to drive sustained profitable growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remains confident, with increased emphasis on outperformance, guidance clarity, and strategic expansion, while analysts' sentiment remains positive and inquisitive on new initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for direct premiums written growth increased from 12%-17% (Q3) to 16%-20% (Q4) for 2026.
  • The company shifted to presenting the underlying combined ratio as the main operating metric, enhancing transparency on controllable performance.
  • Strategic focus expanded with the announcement of California market entry, compared to the prior quarter's generic expansion roadmap.
  • Analysts focused on new state entry and expense ratio sustainability, compared to prior focus on product mix and competitive landscape.
  • The Select product's share of policies in force increased further, and the company continued to report improved expense and loss ratios.
  • Management's tone in Q4 emphasized confidence in strategic execution and scalability, while the previous quarter focused more on consistency and discipline.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged the unpredictability of catastrophe events and included higher-than-average catastrophe losses in 2026 guidance, citing "elevated winter storm activity."
  • The CEO addressed regulatory risks in New York, noting, "we are monitoring these proposals closely and engaging constructively through industry bodies."
  • Analysts inquired about competitive threats in New York and the challenges of entering the California market, prompting management to reiterate confidence in their competitive positioning and disciplined approach.

Final Takeaway

Kingstone Companies enters 2026 with record profitability, a strengthened capital base, and a clear focus on disciplined growth. The company projects direct premiums written to grow 16% to 20% in 2026, while launching into the California market and maintaining its trajectory toward $500 million in direct premiums written by 2029. Management attributes these results to structural improvements, robust product performance, and operating efficiency, emphasizing that guidance for the upcoming year accounts for normalized catastrophe losses and ongoing regulatory developments. The company’s leadership remains confident in its ability to execute its growth roadmap and deliver sustained shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brent crude spikes above $90 as Trump says no deal with Iran except 'unconditional surrender'
data.description:
Brent crude oil surged past $90/bbl Friday after President Trump said there will be no deal ending the U.S.-Israel war against Iran without an "unconditional surrender."

Trump's Truth Social declaration appears to cut off the possibility of quickly finding an off-ramp to end a war that has spread across the Middle East.

Front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for May delivery +6.1% to $90.65/bbl, and front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for April delivery +8.9% to $88.25/bbl.

The widening conflict is sparking fears of a global energy crunch as exporters scramble for routes out of the region.

The near-total shutdown of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has caused storage tanks across the region to fill up, causing refineries to cut capacity, while drones have taken out plants in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, and a Bahrain oil processor came under fire overnight.

The inability to send shipments through Hormuz forced Iraq to start shutting down its biggest oil fields earlier this week, and The Wall Street Journal reported Kuwait has begun cutting production ​at some oil fields ‌after running out of room to ​store its bottled-up ​crude.

Qatar's ​energy minister told the Financial Times that he expects all Persian Gulf energy producers to shut down exports ⁠"within days," which he said could drive oil to $150/bbl.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI)

  • See VRT Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Amazon Connect Health, an AI solution for healthcare, launched
data.description:
Amazon Web Services (AWS) has launched Amazon Connect Health, a healthcare tool powered by artificial intelligence that reduces administrative workload for healthcare providers and accelerates care access for patients.

The agentic AI solution is designed to integrate with the electronic health records to automate administrative tasks such as patient verification, scheduling, and documentation, the company said on Thursday.

Amazon Connect Health is specifically tailored to medical settings, applying AI techniques on healthcare-specific datasets and guidelines, according to a statement from Colleen Aubrey, Senior Vice President of AWS Applied AI Solutions.

The company identified the system as the first purpose-built AWS solution for health care providers, and it noted that its primary care chain, One Medical, has already rolled out Amazon Connect Health.

“The results speak for themselves: ambient documentation now spans more than a million visits, with strong clinician adoption and regular weekly usage,” AWS said, adding that One Medical is expanding its use to medical coding to cut the administrative burden further.

data.title: Why Gap's profitability challenges don't make the stock a sell -- analysts
data.description:
Gap’s (GAP) fourth-quarter results, coupled with an underwhelming outlook for the year, continue to weigh on shares, with the stock losing another 13% in Friday's trading.

After the close on Thursday, Gap (GAP) reported a lower profit from a year ago, while company-wide comparable sales missed expectations, largely due to a double-digit drop in traffic at its struggling Athleta banner.

The results reflected a mixed picture for the retailer as revenue growth remained relatively intact despite fewer stores, while the pressure on profits underscored the company’s difficulty in containing costs, specifically those associated with import tariffs. Gap (GAP) imports nearly half of its merchandise from Southeast Asia.

“Tariff impacts were significant,” Gap CFO Katrina O’Connell said on the company’s earnings call with analysts, acknowledging that tariff rates in 2025 had a “substantial” impact on the company’s profits.

“Specifically, tariffs influenced our fiscal year’s gross and operating margins by approximately 120 basis points and fourth quarter gross and operating margins by approximately 200 basis points,” O’Connell added.

Although the Supreme Court ruled against President Trump’s trade tariffs, a 15% global tariff remains in place and will likely impact Gap (GAP) through the first half of 2026. The company expects a 200 basis point tariff headwind in the first quarter and 100 basis points in the second.

Fortunately, under the direction of CEO Richard Dickson, Gap (GAP) is executing on a strategy to drive profitable growth, says Jefferies’ Corey Tarlowe. Coupled with high-margin opportunities in beauty “that can provide meaningful top- and bottom-line upside over time, Gap remains well-positioned for success,” Tarlowe adds.

Agrees J.P. Morgan’s Matthew Boss, who sees Gap (GAP) at an “inflection point” to support low- to mid-single-digit sales growth and annual margin expansion targeting “historical levels of profitability.”

For now, however, profitability remains an issue for the company, one that Seeking Alpha analyst Daniel Jones, investing group leader for Crude Value Insights, attributes to cost management.

“The issue for the company has less to do with its ability to generate revenue and more to do with its ability to contain costs,” Jones says.

“The 38.10% [gross profit]  that the company achieved in the final quarter of last year fell short of the 38.85% reported the previous year. I know this doesn't seem like much of a disparity. But when applied to the revenue generated by the company during the final quarter, it amounts to an extra $31.8M in pre-tax profits that the company missed out on,” Jones added.

But despite the exposure to tariffs and Athleta challenges, Gap (GAP) remains a Buy for Tarlowe, Boss, and Jones.

Tarlowe points to the company’s “margin resilience despite tariff pressure,” while Boss attributes his Overweight rating to his forecast for 200 basis points of operating expansion in the second half of the year.

“Based on all the data provided at the moment, it is my belief that Gap (GAP) might not be the perfect candidate, but it is one that investors would be wise to take seriously,” Jones advises.
